plan:
- Microcontroller: ESP32-CAM module for camera and processing, powered by a 5V supply.
- Sensors: DHT11 temperature/humidity sensor; integrated ESP32-CAM camera for facial detection.
- Actuators: Green LED (GPIO 4), Red LED (GPIO 12) with PWM for brightness control, SG90 servo motor (GPIO 13) for door control.
- Mechanical: 12"x12"x6" chassis/enclosure (e.g., acrylic, ABS) with mounting hardware (standoffs, screws).
- Custom Parts: 3D-printed mounts for LEDs, DHT11 sensor, and servo motor door mechanism adapter.
- Interconnections: Wiring for LEDs, DHT11, servo, and power supply to ESP32-CAM.

originalPrompt:
Build an energy-efficient Smart Room Project using ESP32-CAM and Arduino IDE.

Requirements:
1. Security & Room Occupancy State: Use ESP32-CAM local facial detection algorithms.
   - Initial State: Room is empty (`roomOccupied = false`), meaning the AC indicator (Red LED) remains strictly OFF.
   - Upon Successful Identification: Trigger a Green LED Indicator (GPIO 4) and open the door via an SG90 Servo motor (GPIO 13). Simultaneously set `roomOccupied = true`.
   - Automatically close/lock the door and turn off the Green LED after exactly 5 seconds.
2. Occupancy-Based AC Loop: Run the weather sensing cycle ONLY if `roomOccupied` is true. If false, keep the Red LED completely off regardless of external temperature.
3. Temperature Tracking Logic (DHT11 on GPIO 14):
   - If Temp > 32°C and room is occupied, set Red LED (GPIO 12) brightness to 100%.
   - If Temp is between 25°C and 32°C and room is occupied, set Red LED to 30% brightness.
   - If Temp < 25°C, drive the Red LED to 0% (OFF).
4. Output Format: Full component schematic blueprint framework, dimensional chassis routing for a 12"x12"x6" build, and optimized firmware source code.

subSteps.title: 3D print all mechanical mounting components and the model door
Prepare enclosure panels, ensuring all mounting holes are present and deburred
Attach M3 standoffs to internal corners of the enclosure panels for structural assembly
Connect DHT11 sensor to ESP32-CAM MCU (VCC, GND, GPIO14)
Wire Green LED indicator to ESP32-CAM MCU (GND, GPIO4)
Wire Red LED AC indicator to ESP32-CAM MCU (GND, GPIO12)
Connect SG90 servo to ESP32-CAM MCU (5V, GND, GPIO13)
Perform continuity check on all wired connections to ensure correct power and data paths
Upload basic test firmware to ESP32-CAM MCU to verify power and communication
Test DHT11 sensor readings by uploading a sample sketch to the ESP32-CAM
Verify Green and Red LED indicator functionality by toggling pins in test firmware
Test SG90 servo movement and range by sending control signals from the ESP32-CAM
Flash final 'Smart Room Automation' firmware to the ESP32-CAM MCU
Mount the ESP32-CAM MCU into its 3D printed mount and secure the mount to the front panel
Mount the DHT11 sensor into its 3D printed mount and secure the mount to the front panel
Install the Green and Red LED indicators into their bezels and mount to the front panel
Attach the SG90 servo to its mount and secure the mount to the front panel
Assemble the small model door with upper and lower hinges, then attach hinges to the front panel
Attach the servo arm door adapter to the SG90 servo and the small model door
Route all electrical wires neatly and secure them with cable ties, ensuring no strain on connections
Close the enclosure using M3 screws to secure all panels together

projectDescription: An ESP32-CAM powers this smart room system, performing facial detection to control a servo-actuated door. It monitors room occupancy and DHT11 temperature, activating green/red LEDs for access and climate indication within a 12"x12"x6" chassis.

imagePromptSnapshot.description:
Project idea: Build an energy-efficient Smart Room Project using ESP32-CAM and Arduino IDE.

Requirements:
1. Security & Room Occupancy State: Use ESP32-CAM local facial detection algorithms.
   - Initial State: Room is empty (`roomOccupied = false`), meaning the AC indicator (Red LED) remains strictly OFF.
   - Upon Successful Identification: Trigger a Green LED Indicator (GPIO 4) and open the door via an SG90 Servo motor (GPIO 13). Simultaneously set `roomOccupied = true`.
   - Automatically close/lock the door and turn off the Green LED after exactly 5 seconds.
2. Occupancy-Based AC Loop: Run the weather sensing cycle ONLY if `roomOccupied` is true. If false, keep the Red LED completely off regardless of external temperature.
3. Temperature Tracking Logic (DHT11 on GPIO 14):
   - If Temp > 32°C and room is occupied, set Red LED (GPIO 12) brightness to 100%.
   - If Temp is between 25°C and 32°C and room is occupied, set Red LED to 30% brightness.
   - If Temp < 25°C, drive the Red LED to 0% (OFF).
4. Output Format: Full component schematic blueprint framework, dimensional chassis routing for a 12"x12"x6" build, and optimized firmware source code.

Design notes: Energy-efficient, Smart Room, Facial detection, Occupancy-based AC, 12x12x6 chassis, Arduino IDE

Electrical components (current): Main Controller (mcu, 40x27x12mm); Room Temp/Humidity Sensor (sensor, 28x12x7mm); 3x actuator: Door Open Indicator LED, AC Status Indicator LED, Door Opening Servo

Mechanical/structural parts (current): 2x misc: M3 Standoffs for Panels, M3 Screws for Panels; 9x 3d_printed: ESP32-CAM Mount, DHT11 Sensor Mount, Green LED Bezel Mount, Red LED Bezel Mount +5 more

Assembly: Front Enclosure Panel → Top Enclosure Panel (M3 screws into M3 standoffs); Front Enclosure Panel → Bottom Enclosure Panel (M3 screws into M3 standoffs); Front Enclosure Panel → Left Enclosure Panel (M3 screws into M3 standoffs); Front Enclosure Panel → Right Enclosure Panel (M3 screws into M3 standoffs); Rear Enclosure Panel → Top Enclosure Panel (M3 screws into M3 standoffs); Rear Enclosure Panel → Bottom Enclosure Panel (M3 screws into M3 standoffs); Rear Enclosure Panel → Left Enclosure Panel (M3 screws into M3 standoffs); Rear Enclosure Panel → Right Enclosure Panel (M3 screws into M3 standoffs); Top Enclosure Panel → Left Enclosure Panel (M3 screws into M3 standoffs); Top Enclosure Panel → Right Enclosure Panel (M3 screws into M3 standoffs); Bottom Enclosure Panel → Left Enclosure Panel (M3 screws into M3 standoffs); Bottom Enclosure Panel → Right Enclosure Panel (M3 screws into M3 standoffs); ESP32-CAM Mount → Front Enclosure Panel (M3 bolts); DHT11 Sensor Mount → Front Enclosure Panel (M3 bolts); Green LED Bezel Mount → Front Enclosure Panel (panel mount with nut) (+13 more connections)

tools: M2 hex key or small Phillips screwdriver
Hot glue gun or Super glue
Drill (optional, for enclosure holes if not pre-drilled)
assumptions: Arduino IDE installed and configured for ESP32-CAM
Familiarity with basic electronics wiring practices
USB-to-Serial converter for ESP32-CAM programming (if not using onboard USB-C programmer)
